formulas.name: Normal (Axial) Stress
assumptions: Axial load is applied along the centroidal axis
Stress is uniformly distributed across the section
formulas.limitations: Valid only away from points of load application or abrupt section change (Saint-Venant's principle); does not apply to eccentric loading.
typicalApplications: axially loaded rods, bars, and cables

formulas.name: Axial Deformation
formulas.derivation: From Hooke's Law, sigma = E*epsilon, and epsilon = delta/L, and sigma = P/A. Substituting: P/A = E*(delta/L), solving for delta gives delta = PL/(AE).
assumptions: Material is linear-elastic (stress within the proportional limit)
Constant P, A, and E along the length (or summed/integrated piecewise if not)
formulas.limitations: Not valid beyond the proportional limit; for varying P, A, or E, integrate delta = ∫P(x)dx/(A(x)E(x)) over the length.

formulas.name: Polar Moment of Inertia — Solid Circular Section
formulas.limitations: Solid circular cross-section only. For a hollow circular shaft, J = pi*(d_o^4 - d_i^4)/32.

formulas.name: Torsional Shear Stress

variables.name: Radial distance from the centroidal axis

formulas.derivation: Maximum shear stress occurs at the outer fiber, r = d/2, giving the equivalent closed form tau_max = 16T/(pi*d^3) for a solid circular shaft.
assumptions: Circular cross-section so plane sections remain plane and shear strain varies linearly with radius
formulas.limitations: Applies to circular (solid or hollow) sections only; non-circular sections warp and require different theory.

formulas.name: Angle of Twist
assumptions: Constant T, J, and G along the shaft length (or summed piecewise if not)
formulas.limitations: Result is in radians; multiply by 180/pi to convert to degrees.
typicalApplications: torsional compatibility in statically indeterminate shafts

formulas.name: Moment of Inertia — Rectangular Section

variables.name: Moment of inertia about the centroidal axis
Section width (perpendicular to the bending axis)
Section depth (parallel to the bending axis)

formulas.limitations: Solid rectangular cross-section about its centroidal (horizontal) axis only. h is the dimension parallel to the direction of bending.

formulas.name: Flexure Formula (Bending Stress)

variables.name: Bending (flexural) stress at distance c from the neutral axis
Distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber
Moment of inertia of the section about the neutral axis

formulas.derivation: From the linear strain assumption (plane sections remain plane) and Hooke's Law, stress varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis; equilibrium of the internal moment with the stress distribution gives sigma = My/I, maximized at y = c.
assumptions: Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory)
Beam is straight and prismatic; deflections are small
formulas.limitations: Neutral axis must pass through the section centroid (true for pure bending of homogeneous, symmetric sections only).
typicalApplications: beam section design and capacity checks
